perturbation.original_text: The Parties will (and shall ensure that their respective Representatives will) keep the ConfidentialInformation confidential and will not, without the prior written consent of the other Party, disclose anyConfidential Information in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, and will not use ConfidentialInformation, directly or indirectly, for other than evaluating a possible Transaction. The Parties shall Reciprocal Confidential Non-Disclosure Agreement 3.12 of 3make all reasonable, necessary or appropriate efforts to safeguard the Confidential Information fromdisclosure to anyone other than as permitted hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will endeavor to keep the Confidential Information confidential, and without express written consent, will try not to disclose any Confidential Information in any manner, and will attempt not to use Confidential Information other than evaluating a possible Transaction. The Parties will make efforts they consider appropriate to safeguard the Confidential Information from disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by replacing definitive terms like 'keep confidential' with 'endeavor to keep confidential' and 'will not disclose' with 'will try not to disclose'. The original text created an unambiguous obligation to safeguard the information. By changing the terms from 'shall' to weaker terms such as 'endeavor', 'try', and 'efforts they consider appropriate' the responsibility becomes vague and based on subjective interpretation, thus creating an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts PIPEDA (applicable in Ontario, where the contract is governed) because Principle 4.7 requires organizations to protect personal information with security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. Using vague terms like 'endeavor to keep confidential' and 'efforts they consider appropriate' fails to meet the requirement of providing specific and adequate security safeguards, leaving the obligation open to interpretation and potentially resulting in inadequate protection of personal information. It undermines the specificity required for compliance with PIPEDA.

Marginal note: Writing requirements 41 Â A requirement under a provision of a federal law for a document to be in writing is satisfied by an electronic document if (a) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; and (b) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Original documents 42 Â A requirement under a provision of a federal law for a document to be in its original form is satisfied by an electronic document if (a) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; (b) Â the electronic document contains a secure electronic signature that was added when the electronic document was first generated in its final form and that can be used to verify that the electronic document has not been changed since that time; and (c) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Signatures 43 Â Subject to sectionsÂ 44 to 46, a requirement under a provision of a federal law for a signature is satisfied by an electronic signature if (a) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; and (b) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Statements made under oath 44 Â A statement required to be made under oath or solemn affirmation under a provision of a federal law may be made in electronic form if (a) Â the person who makes the statement signs it with that personâs secure electronic signature; (b) Â the person before whom the statement was made, and who is authorized to take statements under oath or solemn affirmation, signs it with that personâs secure electronic signature; (c) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; and (d) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Statements declaring truth, etc. 45 Â A statement required to be made under a provision of a federal law declaring or certifying that any information given by a person making the statement is true, accurate or complete may be made in electronic form if (a) Â the person signs it with that personâs secure electronic signature; (b) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; and (c) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Witnessed signatures 46 Â A requirement under a provision of a federal law for a signature to be witnessed is satisfied with respect to an electronic document if (a) Â each signatory and each witness signs the electronic document with their secure electronic signature; (b) Â the federal law or the provision is listed in ScheduleÂ 2 or 3; and (c) Â the regulations respecting the application of this section to the provision have been complied with. Marginal note: Copies 47 Â A requirement under a provision of a federal law for one or more copies of a document to be submitted is satisfied by the submission of an
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by replacing definitive terms with weaker, less enforceable language. The original text uses 'will' and 'shall ensure', creating a clear obligation. The modified text uses 'endeavor to keep confidential', 'try not to disclose', and 'efforts they consider appropriate', weakening the obligation and making it subjective. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts PIPEDA because Principle 4.7 requires specific and adequate security safeguards. While the provided snippets do not directly refer to Principle 4.7 or the specific terms 'endeavor' or 'try', the Law Explanation connects this change to the need for specific safeguards, which is undermined by the ambiguous language, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that the other Party would not have an adequate remedy at lawand would be irreparably harmed if any of the provisions of this Agreement were not performed inaccordance with their specific terms or were otherwise breached. Accordingly, the Parties agree thateither Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent breaches of this Agreement and to specificallyenforce the terms and provisions hereof in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled at lawor in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the other Party may not have an adequate remedy at law and may be harmed if the provisions of this Agreement were not generally followed or were possibly breached. Accordingly, the Parties suggest that either Party could be entitled to injunctive relief to address potential breaches of this Agreement, along with any other remedy available.
perturbation.explanation: The original text asserts the irreparable harm and entitlement to injunctive relief. The modified version uses weaker language ('may', 'generally followed', 'possibly breached', 'could be entitled') making the entitlement to injunctive relief uncertain and discretionary. This undermines the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43
perturbation.law_citation: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 102
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause created a clear pathway to injunctive relief. The modified version replaces this with a suggestion, which introduces ambiguity as to whether a court would grant injunctive relief. Section 102 of the Ontario Courts of Justice Act provides the court the ability to grant injunctive relief if it is just and equitable to do so. The modified wording weakens the claim for injunctive relief, potentially causing a court to deny such relief if the right to seek it is not clearly established in the contract.

perturbation.justification: The original text established a clear entitlement to injunctive relief, while the changed text weakens this entitlement by using terms like 'may' and 'could be entitled'. This introduces ambiguity, making it uncertain whether a court would grant injunctive relief. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that the modified version undermines the enforceability of the agreement. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the weakened wording potentially causes a court to deny injunctive relief if the right to seek it is not clearly established in the contract. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Courts of Justice Act, which, according to the Law Explanation, is the basis for granting injunctive relief, further emphasizing the impact of weakening the claim.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will not, and will direct their respective representatives and associates not to, disclose toany other person that the Confidential Information has been made available to them, that discussions ornegotiations are taking place concerning a potential Transaction or any of the terms, conditions or otherfacts with respect to any such Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will discourage their representatives from disclosing, to any other person, that Confidential Information has been made available, or that discussions are possibly occurring concerning a Transaction, except as needed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict obligation not to disclose. The modified version uses 'discourage' which weakens the obligation, and introduces 'except as needed' which creates a significant ambiguity. Who determines what is 'needed'? Under what circumstances? This undermines the confidential nature of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA s. 5(3) states than an organization may collect, use or disclose personal information only for purposes that a reasonable person would consider are appropriate in the circumstances. The modification includes an 'except as needed' clause that makes the non-disclosure provision ambiguous and potentially conflicting with PIPEDA. The 'except as needed' clause might be interpreted as allowing disclosure of confidential information, including personal information, for purposes that are not reasonable or appropriate. This creates a loophole that undermines the privacy protections intended by PIPEDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector (continued) DIVISION 1.1 Breaches of Security Safeguards Marginal note: Report to Commissioner 10.1 Â (1) Â An organization shall report to the Commissioner any breach of security safeguards involving personal information under its control if it is reasonable in the circumstances to believe that the breach creates a real risk of significant harm to an individual. Marginal note: Report requirements (2) Â The report shall contain the prescribed information and shall be made in the prescribed form and manner as soon as feasible after the organization determines that the breach has occurred. Marginal note: Notification to individual (3) Â Unless otherwise prohibited by law, an organization shall notify an individual of any breach of security safeguards involving the individualâs personal information under the organizationâs control if it is reasonable in the circumstances to believe that the breach creates a real risk of significant harm to the individual. Marginal note: Contents of notification (4) Â The notification shall contain sufficient information to allow the individual to understand the significance to them of the breach and to take steps, if any are possible, to reduce the risk of harm that could result from it or to mitigate that harm. It shall also contain any other prescribed information. Marginal note: Form and manner (5) Â The notification shall be conspicuous and shall be given directly to the individual in the prescribed form and manner, except in prescribed circumstances, in which case it shall be given indirectly in the prescribed form and manner. Marginal note: Time to give notification (6) Â The notification shall be given as soon as feasible after the organization determines that the breach has occurred. Marginal note: Definition of significant harm (7) Â For the purpose of this section, significant harm includes bodily harm, humiliation, damage to reputation or relationships, loss of employment, business or professional opportunities, financial loss, identity theft, negative effects on the credit record and damage to or loss of property. Marginal note: Real risk of significant harm â factors (8) Â The factors that are relevant to determining whether a breach of security safeguards creates a real risk of significant harm to the individual include (a) Â the sensitivity of the personal information involved in the breach; (b) Â the probability that the personal information has been, is being or will be misused; and (c) Â any other prescribed factor. 2015, c. 32, s. 10 Marginal note: Notification to organizations 10.2 Â (1) Â An organization that notifies an individual of
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'discourage' instead of 'will not,' weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Additionally, the 'except as needed' clause creates a significant loophole, as it doesn't specify who determines what is 'needed' or under what circumstances disclosure is permissible. The LLM Law Explanation points to PIPEDA s. 5(3), which emphasizes that personal information should only be disclosed for purposes a reasonable person would consider appropriate. The 'except as needed' clause, without further clarification, could allow for disclosure of confidential information, including personal information, for unreasonable or inappropriate purposes, thus potentially conflicting with PIPEDA. The scraped law snippet 2, titled 'Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act,' and specifically section 10.1 regarding breaches of security safeguards, reinforces the importance of protecting personal information. While snippet 2 focuses on reporting breaches, the modification introduces ambiguity that could lead to such breaches by allowing undefined exceptions to the non-disclosure agreement, indirectly undermining the safeguards contemplated by PIPEDA.
